skill.name: test-driven-development
skill.description: Guide AI coding agents through strict TDD by writing a failing test before implementation, then making it pass and refactoring.
skill.summary: Apply red-green-refactor discipline to features, bug fixes, refactors, and behavior changes.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Ruby backticks, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance are false positives from Markdown fences and test examples. No evidence found for prompt injection, credential access, network exfiltration, or malicious commands. Medium risk remains because the skill strongly instructs agents to delete code when TDD order is violated.

medium_findings.title: Potentially Destructive Workflow Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs agents to delete code and start over when implementation precedes tests. This is not malicious, but it can cause user data loss if applied without confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deletion guidance is explicit and repeated in the file. The impact depends on agent behavior and user confirmation controls, so confidence is high but not absolute.

low_findings.title: Local Test Command Execution
low_findings.description: The skill tells agents to run local test commands such as npm test. This is expected for a coding skill, but project test scripts can execute arbitrary local commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are visible in Bash code blocks and are limited to local test execution. The risk is contextual because test scripts belong to the target project.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance hits do not show executable code or malicious intent. They are Markdown formatting and ordinary testing examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown code fences or instructional snippets. No shell interpolation, crypto implementation, scanning command, or outbound endpoint was found.

content.value_statement: Teams often ship changes without proof that tests catch the intended behavior. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through strict red-green-refactor work.
actual_capabilities: Guides agents to write a failing test before implementation.
Defines red, green, and refactor checks for each change.
Explains how to verify that failures are meaningful.
Gives examples for bug fixes, retries, validation, and mocks.
Identifies testing anti-patterns around mock behavior and test-only APIs.
Provides a completion checklist for tested changes.
limitations: Does not create tests automatically without project context.
Requires a runnable test framework in the target repository.
May be too strict for prototypes or generated code.
Deletion guidance needs user confirmation in shared workspaces.

use_cases.title: Add a Feature with Tests First
use_cases.description: Use the skill to define one expected behavior, watch the test fail, then implement only enough code to pass.

use_cases.title: Fix a Regression Safely
use_cases.description: Use the skill to reproduce the bug with a failing test before changing production code.

use_cases.title: Review Test Design
use_cases.description: Use the anti-pattern guidance to spot mocks, test-only methods, and incomplete fixtures that weaken confidence.

prompt_templates.title: Start a TDD Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the test-driven-development skill for this feature. First propose one failing test for the next behavior, then wait before implementing.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a Bug with a Failing Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the test-driven-development skill to reproduce this bug. Write the smallest failing test, explain the expected failure, then implement the fix.

prompt_templates.title: Check Existing Test Quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the test-driven-development skill and testing anti-patterns reference. Review these tests for mock behavior, missing failure checks, and weak assertions.

prompt_templates.title: Guide a Complex Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the test-driven-development skill to plan this refactor. Identify behavior tests first, sequence red-green-refactor steps, and avoid adding behavior during cleanup.

output_examples.input: Add retry support for failed operations.
output_examples.output: The agent proposes a failing retry behavior test, confirms the failure reason, implements the smallest retry loop, and then checks all tests.
The agent writes a failing empty-email test, verifies the missing error, adds minimal validation, and confirms the regression test passes.

output_examples.input: Review tests that assert on mock components.
output_examples.output: The agent flags assertions that prove mock presence only and recommends testing visible behavior or using real components.

best_practices: Run the new test and confirm it fails for the expected reason.
Implement the smallest change that makes the failing test pass.
Refactor only after tests are green and behavior is protected.
anti_patterns: Writing implementation code before a failing test exists.
Testing mocks instead of user-visible or domain behavior.
Adding production methods that exist only for test cleanup.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance that may lead an agent to run local test commands with user or tool approval.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. Local test scripts can run arbitrary project-defined commands in any coding environment.

faq.question: Can I use it for bug fixes?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill recommends writing a failing regression test before changing production code.

faq.question: Can I skip TDD for prototypes?
faq.answer: The skill allows exceptions for throwaway prototypes, generated code, and configuration files with human approval.

faq.question: Does it support mock-heavy tests?
faq.answer: It discourages unnecessary mocks and recommends testing real behavior whenever possible.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing changes?
faq.answer: Users should review the test failure, final passing tests, file diffs, and any deleted code.
